title: The friendship conducted in a language neither of us owns
summary: Argue whether a close friendship carried out entirely in a third language — one that is native to neither person — is a thinner thing than friendship in a mother tongue, or whether the shared limitation creates an intimacy of its own kind.
topic: Whether friendship in a borrowed common language is diminished or differently rich
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay about a friendship you conduct, or could imagine conducting, in a language native to neither person — the classic case of two foreigners meeting in a third country and sharing only, say, English. Take a genuine position on whether such a friendship is impoverished by the absence of a mother tongue or enriched by the equality the borrowed language enforces. Anticipate and answer the strongest objection to your view. Use concession, deliberate cohesion devices, and idiomatic precision; avoid a bland 'both sides' ending and commit to a qualified claim.
hintStarters: It is easy to assume that two people who share no mother tongue are condemned to a friendship of subtitles, but…
The objection I take most seriously is that real intimacy lives in the untranslatable — in the joke that only works in dialect — and yet…
What the borrowed language takes away in nuance it returns, oddly, in the form of…

sampleText:
My closest friend in the city where I now live and I have known each other for nine years, and in all that time we have not exchanged a single sentence in either of our mother tongues. She grew up speaking Tagalog; I grew up speaking Hungarian; we met in a shared kitchen in a building full of people who had arrived from everywhere, and we have conducted the whole of our friendship in English, which belongs to neither of us. It is easy to assume that two people who share no mother tongue are condemned to a friendship of subtitles — accurate enough for daily life, but always a translation of the real thing happening somewhere offstage. I want to argue that this assumption is wrong, or at least far too quick.

The case against me is strong, and I should put it at its strongest. Intimacy, the objection runs, lives precisely in the untranslatable: in the joke that only works in dialect, in the word your grandmother used that has no equivalent, in the half-sentence a lifelong friend completes before you finish it. A friendship in a borrowed tongue is barred from all of that. It runs at the speed of the slower speaker; it loses the puns; it cannot reach for the phrase that would land exactly, and must settle, again and again, for the phrase that lands nearly. By this account, my friend and I are not so much close as efficiently approximate, two people who have learned to read each other's gestures because the words will not do the work.

There is truth in this, and I will not pretend otherwise. There are jokes I have given up trying to make. But the objection mistakes the absence of a shared mother tongue for the absence of depth, and these are not the same thing. Consider what the borrowed language removes along with the nuance: it removes the home advantage. Neither of us can take the upper hand by being more fluent, more native, more obviously at ease. We meet, every time, on ground that is foreign to us both, and that enforced equality turns out to do something a mother tongue rarely does. It makes us slow down. It makes us check. When neither party can assume understanding, both parties keep asking — do you mean this, or that? — and a friendship in which the question is always live is not a thinner friendship. It is, in one specific and underrated respect, a more honest one.

The deliberateness cuts the other way, too. Because the words cost us both some effort, we do not waste them. I have noticed that I lie to her less than I lie to friends in Hungarian — not because I am a better person in English, but because the language gives me nowhere to hide. The fluent evasions, the practised charm, the sentence that sounds like candour and is really a performance of it: all of that requires a fluency I do not have in this tongue. Stripped of my rhetorical home turf, I am reduced to saying, more or less, the thing I actually mean, in the plainest words I can find. That reduction felt like a loss for about a year. It has come to feel like the opposite.

None of this makes the borrowed language a superior instrument. It would be absurd to claim that two people would be closer for never sharing a first language; plainly, my friend and I would have access to more if we did. The honest claim is narrower and, I think, more interesting: that the shared limitation generates its own kind of intimacy, one built not on the shorthand of a common past but on the patience of a common present — the daily, mutual labour of making sure we have understood. That intimacy is real. It is not a consolation prize for the closeness we cannot have; it is a different closeness, available only to people who have nothing easy in common and decide to build something anyway.

So I hold to the qualified case. A friendship in a language neither party owns gives up the untranslatable and keeps the unhurried; it forfeits the pun and gains the question; it trades the comfort of being effortlessly understood for the rarer experience of being carefully understood. Whether that is a fair trade is not, in the end, a general question. For the two of us, across nine years and one borrowed language, it has been more than fair. It has been the friendship of my adult life.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Steel-manning: stating the objection at its strongest
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'The case against me is strong, and I should put it at its strongest' rồi cả đoạn trình bày lời phản bác bằng giọng đầy thiện chí ('Intimacy, the objection runs, lives precisely in the untranslatable'). Việc tự nêu phản đề mạnh nhất trước khi bác lại — gọi là steel-manning — làm lập luận đáng tin hơn nhiều so với việc dựng một bù nhìn dễ đánh. Đây là kỹ thuật lập luận cốt lõi của C1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The line 'The case against me is strong, and I should put it at its strongest', followed by a fair, fully voiced objection ('Intimacy, the objection runs, lives precisely in the untranslatable'), is steel-manning: presenting the opposing view at its most persuasive before answering it. Doing so makes the argument far more credible than attacking a weak straw man — a core C1 argumentative technique.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Parallel antithesis as cohesion in the conclusion
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn kết dùng chuỗi đối lập song hành: 'gives up the untranslatable and keeps the unhurried; it forfeits the pun and gains the question; it trades the comfort of being effortlessly understood for... being carefully understood'. Cấu trúc 'X nhưng Y' lặp lại nhịp nhàng vừa liên kết các ý vừa nén toàn bộ lập luận đánh đổi vào một nhịp điệu cân xứng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The conclusion uses a chain of parallel antitheses: 'gives up the untranslatable and keeps the unhurried; it forfeits the pun and gains the question; it trades the comfort of being effortlessly understood for... being carefully understood.' The repeated 'gives up X / keeps Y' rhythm both binds the ideas together and compresses the whole trade-off argument into a balanced cadence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Người bạn thân nhất của tôi ở thành phố tôi đang sống và tôi đã quen nhau chín năm, và suốt chừng ấy thời gian chúng tôi chưa từng trao đổi dù chỉ một câu bằng tiếng mẹ đẻ của một trong hai người. Cô ấy lớn lên nói tiếng Tagalog; tôi lớn lên nói tiếng Hungary; chúng tôi gặp nhau trong một căn bếp chung ở một toà nhà đầy những người đã đến từ khắp nơi, và chúng tôi đã duy trì toàn bộ tình bạn bằng tiếng Anh, thứ tiếng không thuộc về ai trong hai. Thật dễ cho rằng hai người không có chung tiếng mẹ đẻ thì bị kết án vào một tình bạn của phụ đề — đủ chính xác cho sinh hoạt hằng ngày, nhưng luôn chỉ là bản dịch của điều thực sự đang diễn ra ở đâu đó sau cánh gà. Tôi muốn lập luận rằng giả định này là sai, hoặc ít nhất là quá vội vàng.

Lập luận chống lại tôi rất mạnh, và tôi nên trình bày nó ở dạng mạnh nhất. Sự thân mật, theo lời phản bác, nằm chính ở cái không thể dịch được: ở câu đùa chỉ ăn nhập trong tiếng địa phương, ở từ mà bà bạn từng dùng vốn chẳng có từ tương đương, ở nửa câu mà một người bạn tri kỷ hoàn thành nốt trước cả khi bạn nói hết. Một tình bạn bằng ngôn ngữ đi mượn bị chặn khỏi tất cả những thứ đó. Nó chạy ở tốc độ của người nói chậm hơn; nó đánh mất những lối chơi chữ; nó không thể với tới cụm từ trúng đích hoàn toàn, mà phải hài lòng, hết lần này đến lần khác, với cụm từ chỉ gần trúng. Theo cách lý giải này, bạn tôi và tôi không hẳn là thân thiết mà là gần đúng một cách hiệu quả, hai người đã học cách đọc cử chỉ của nhau vì lời nói không kham nổi việc đó.

Có phần đúng trong điều này, và tôi sẽ không giả vờ ngược lại. Có những câu đùa tôi đã bỏ cuộc không cố pha nữa. Nhưng lời phản bác nhầm việc thiếu một tiếng mẹ đẻ chung với việc thiếu chiều sâu, mà hai thứ này không giống nhau. Hãy xem ngôn ngữ đi mượn lấy đi điều gì cùng với sắc thái: nó tước đi lợi thế sân nhà. Không ai trong hai có thể giành thế thượng phong bằng việc trôi chảy hơn, bản xứ hơn, thoải mái hơn một cách lộ liễu. Mỗi lần, chúng tôi gặp nhau trên mảnh đất xa lạ với cả hai, và sự bình đẳng bị áp đặt ấy hoá ra làm được điều mà tiếng mẹ đẻ hiếm khi làm. Nó khiến chúng tôi chậm lại. Nó khiến chúng tôi kiểm tra lại. Khi không bên nào có thể mặc định là đã hiểu, cả hai bên cứ hỏi mãi — ý cậu là cái này, hay cái kia? — và một tình bạn nơi câu hỏi luôn còn bỏ ngỏ không phải là một tình bạn mỏng hơn. Ở một khía cạnh cụ thể và bị xem nhẹ, nó là một tình bạn trung thực hơn.

Sự cân nhắc ấy còn cắt theo chiều ngược lại nữa. Vì lời nói khiến cả hai phải tốn chút công sức, chúng tôi không phí phạm chúng. Tôi để ý rằng tôi nói dối cô ấy ít hơn so với khi nói dối bạn bè bằng tiếng Hungary — không phải vì tôi là người tốt hơn khi nói tiếng Anh, mà vì ngôn ngữ ấy không cho tôi chỗ nào để trốn. Những lời né tránh trôi chảy, sự duyên dáng đã luyện thành thục, câu nghe như sự thẳng thắn nhưng thật ra là màn diễn của sự thẳng thắn: tất cả những thứ đó đòi hỏi một độ trôi chảy mà tôi không có trong thứ tiếng này. Bị tước mất lãnh địa hùng biện quen thuộc của mình, tôi bị rút gọn về việc nói, ít nhiều, đúng điều tôi thực sự nghĩ, bằng những lời mộc mạc nhất tôi tìm được. Sự rút gọn ấy có cảm giác như một mất mát trong khoảng một năm. Rồi nó dần có cảm giác ngược lại.

Không điều nào trong số này biến ngôn ngữ đi mượn thành một công cụ ưu việt. Thật phi lý khi cho rằng hai người sẽ thân thiết hơn nhờ chưa từng có chung một ngôn ngữ thứ nhất; rõ ràng bạn tôi và tôi sẽ tiếp cận được nhiều hơn nếu có. Khẳng định trung thực thì hẹp hơn và, tôi nghĩ, thú vị hơn: rằng sự giới hạn chung tạo ra một kiểu thân mật của riêng nó, được dựng không phải trên lối nói tắt của một quá khứ chung mà trên sự kiên nhẫn của một hiện tại chung — cái công sức chung, hằng ngày, của việc bảo đảm rằng chúng tôi đã hiểu nhau. Sự thân mật ấy là thật. Nó không phải giải an ủi cho sự gần gũi mà chúng tôi không thể có; nó là một sự gần gũi khác, chỉ dành cho những người không có điểm chung nào dễ dàng mà vẫn quyết tâm dựng nên một thứ gì đó.

Vậy nên tôi giữ vững lập luận có điều kiện này. Một tình bạn bằng ngôn ngữ không thuộc về ai trong hai từ bỏ cái không thể dịch và giữ lại sự thong thả; nó đánh mất lối chơi chữ và thu về câu hỏi; nó đổi sự dễ chịu của việc được thấu hiểu không tốn sức lấy trải nghiệm hiếm hoi hơn của việc được thấu hiểu một cách cẩn trọng. Liệu đó có phải một sự đánh đổi công bằng hay không, rốt cuộc, không phải một câu hỏi chung chung. Với hai chúng tôi, qua chín năm và một ngôn ngữ đi mượn, nó đã hơn cả công bằng. Nó đã là tình bạn của cả đời người trưởng thành của tôi.

mcQuestions.prompt: What position does the essay ultimately argue?
options: That friendship in a borrowed language is always superior to friendship in a mother tongue.
That the two friends should learn each other's mother tongues.
That language has no effect on friendship at all.
That a friendship in a language neither party owns gives up some things and gains others, and for the writer the trade has been more than fair.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion is a qualified case: it 'gives up the untranslatable and keeps the unhurried', and for the writer 'it has been more than fair'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's balanced, committed claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay handle the strongest objection to its view?
options: It states the objection at its strongest — that intimacy lives in the untranslatable — then concedes its partial truth before answering it.
It ignores the objection entirely.
It dismisses the objection as stupid.
It agrees with the objection and abandons its own thesis.
mcQuestions.explanation: The writer says 'I should put it at its strongest', voices it fully, grants 'There is truth in this', then rebuts it — steel-manning.
mcQuestions.hint: What does the writer do before rebutting the opposing view?

mcQuestions.prompt: What unexpected benefit does the borrowed language provide, according to the essay?
options: It makes both friends sound more intelligent.
It removes the 'home advantage', forcing equality, slowness, and constant checking that make the friendship more honest.
It lets them avoid talking about difficult topics.
It allows them to switch languages whenever convenient.
mcQuestions.explanation: The third paragraph argues the borrowed language 'removes the home advantage' and creates an enforced equality where 'the question is always live'.
mcQuestions.hint: What does meeting 'on ground that is foreign to us both' produce?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best demonstrates the concession the prompt requires?
options: There are good and bad sides to every friendship.
The objection is completely wrong about everything.
There is truth in this, and I will not pretend otherwise; but the objection mistakes the absence of a shared mother tongue for the absence of depth.
Friendship is friendship no matter what language you use.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B grants the objection's force ('there is truth in this') before drawing a sharp distinction — a genuine concession, not flat 'both sides'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the line that admits the opponent is partly right before pushing back.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the writer say he lies to his friend less than to Hungarian-speaking friends?
options: Because he is morally better in English.
Because his friend can always tell when he lies.
Because lying is forbidden in their friendship.
Because his limited fluency in the borrowed language denies him the practised evasions and charm that fluency makes possible.
mcQuestions.explanation: He explains it is 'not because I am a better person' but because the language 'gives me nowhere to hide' from the fluent evasions.
mcQuestions.hint: What does he lack in the borrowed language that enables polished lies?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel antithesis, as in the grammar notes?
options: It forfeits the pun and gains the question; it trades the comfort of being effortlessly understood for the rarer experience of being carefully understood.
They met in a shared kitchen many years ago.
English is spoken in many countries today.
Learning a language takes a great deal of time and effort.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B sets matched 'gives up X / gains Y' clauses in balanced opposition, the antithesis the notes describe.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for balanced clauses of the form 'loses X, gains Y'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the best cohesion to follow 'The case against me is strong, and I should put it at its strongest.'?
options: English has a famously large vocabulary.
Intimacy, the objection runs, lives precisely in the untranslatable: in the joke that only works in dialect.
Anyway, the kitchen they met in was quite small.
In today's interconnected world, many languages are spoken.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B delivers the strong objection the previous sentence promised; the others break the thread.
mcQuestions.hint: The sentence promises to state the objection — what should follow?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence matches the essay's argumentative, idiomatically precise register?
options: Borrowed-language friendships are honestly just kind of mid.
It is hereby submitted that interlingual amity is suboptimal.
Stripped of my rhetorical home turf, I am reduced to saying, more or less, the thing I actually mean.
Everybody knows you can't be real friends without the same language.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and idiomatic ('home turf', 'reduced to'); A is slang, C is jargon, D is a sweeping claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Match the controlled, idiomatic, committed voice.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to 'condemned to a friendship of subtitles'?
options: Forced to watch foreign films together.
Required to write everything down before speaking.
Sentenced to silence because they cannot communicate.
Doomed to a friendship that is only ever a rough, lagging translation of some realer thing happening elsewhere.
mcQuestions.explanation: The image suggests a friendship reduced to imperfect translation — 'always a translation of the real thing happening somewhere offstage'.
mcQuestions.hint: What do subtitles do, and why would that be a limitation?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the rhetorical function of the final two sentences about 'nine years and one borrowed language'?
options: They ground the abstract argument in a specific, committed verdict — refusing generality and personally endorsing the trade.
They retreat into a vague 'both sides' position.
They admit the whole essay was wrong.
They promise the friends will switch to a shared mother tongue.
mcQuestions.explanation: The close insists the question 'is not a general question' and commits: 'It has been the friendship of my adult life.'
mcQuestions.hint: Does the ending stay abstract or commit to a personal verdict?

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the writer call the closeness 'a different closeness' rather than a 'consolation prize'?
options: Because he is bitter about not sharing a mother tongue.
Because he insists the intimacy is genuine in its own right — built on shared patience, not a second-best substitute for 'real' closeness.
Because consolation prizes are always worthless.
Because he plans to end the friendship soon.
mcQuestions.explanation: He says it 'is not a consolation prize' but 'a different closeness, available only to people who have nothing easy in common'.
mcQuestions.hint: Is he calling it a lesser version, or a distinct kind?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is grammatical, on-topic, and matches the essay's tone?
options: Tbh the no-shared-language thing is overrated honestly.
It is incumbent upon both interlocutors to optimise comprehension.
When neither party can assume understanding, both parties keep asking — do you mean this, or that?
Everyone knows you need the same language to ever be close.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and on-topic; A is slang, C is jargon, D is an unsupported generalisation.
mcQuestions.hint: Match the careful, concrete, argumentative voice.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the language gives me nowhere to hide' mean in context?
options: That the friend can read his mind.
That he is afraid of his friend.
That the language is too simple to express anything.
That his limited fluency strips away the rhetorical cover that would let him perform candour rather than speak plainly.
mcQuestions.explanation: Without fluent evasions he must say 'more or less, the thing I actually mean, in the plainest words I can find'.
mcQuestions.hint: What 'hiding' does fluency normally make possible?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's distinction between two kinds of intimacy?
options: Built not on the shorthand of a common past but on the patience of a common present.
All friendships are basically the same underneath.
Friendship requires years of shared history to mean anything.
Patience is overrated in close relationships.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B names the contrast at the heart of the argument: shorthand-of-the-past versus patience-of-the-present.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option contrasts a shared past with a shared present?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option preserves the essay's qualified claim rather than overstating it?
options: Borrowed languages always make friendships deeper than mother tongues.
None of this makes the borrowed language a superior instrument; the honest claim is narrower.
A shared mother tongue is the only thing that matters in friendship.
Language differences make genuine friendship impossible.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B explicitly refuses the strong claim and narrows it — the hedged stance the prompt demands.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the line that limits its own conclusion.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to 'a friendship in which the question is always live is not a thinner friendship'?
options: Friendships with many arguments are healthier.
Friends should always interrogate each other.
A friendship where understanding is never simply assumed, and so is always being checked, is not a shallower friendship for that.
Live questions make friendships exhausting.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The question is always live' means understanding is constantly re-confirmed; the writer argues this depth, not thinness.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean for 'the question' of understanding to stay 'live'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a relationship of yours shaped by some shared limitation — a barrier of language, distance, time, or circumstance that you both had to work around. Did the limitation only subtract from the relationship, or did it quietly produce something — a habit, an honesty, a kind of care — that an easier relationship might never have demanded?
